coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots as nanoprobes for sensitive and selective determination of mercury (II) in surface waters
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Hua, Jianhao
Yang, Jian
Zhu, Yan
Zhao, Chunxi
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: A novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was successfully prepared through one-step green hydrothermal method using polyacrylamide as carbon source. The prepared CQDs were characterized using TEM, XRD, XPS, FT-IR, UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The CQDs was demonstrated as nanoprobes for mercury ion detection, moreover, it demonstrated excitation-dependent and superior stability in acidic and alkaline media. Besides, the probe exhibited a good linearity range (0.25–50μM) and a low detection limit (13.48nM). These attractive properties indicated that this novel CQDs can adapt to a variety of complex pH environment, which had extensive prospect and promising application for detection of mercury ions in complex water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Polyacrylamide
Fluorescence quenching
Mercury ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization for the Determination of Hg2+
Sensitivity and Selectivity Measurements and Possible Fluorescence Quenching Mechanism
Hg2+ Measurement in Real Waters Samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Processing diagram for the synthesis and application of CQDs.

(A1) The TEM image of the CQDs (inset: HRTEM image). (A2) EDS pattern of CQDs (B) FT-IR spectra of CQDs. (C) XPS spectra of CQDs. (D) N1s high-resolution XPS spectra. (E) O1s high-resolution XPS spectra. (F) XRD patterns of the CQDs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs. (B) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CQDs. (C) Fluorescence spectra of the CQDs with variations of excitation wavelength.

(A) The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs (B) The optimization of reaction time. (C) A linear correlation of F/F0 values versus the concentration of Hg2+ over the range from 0.25 to 50μM.
(A)The fluorescence response of the CQDs upon the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+. (B) The fluorescence quenching effect of different metal ions.
Determination of mercury ion in the water samples. (n =3).

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots as nanoprobes for sensitive and selective determination of mercury (II) in surface waters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Central Monitoring Center of Kunming City, Yunnan Province 650228, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Central Monitoring Center of Kunming City

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was successfully prepared through one-step green hydrothermal method using polyacrylamide as carbon source. The prepared CQDs were characterized using TEM, XRD, XPS, FT-IR, UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The CQDs was demonstrated as nanoprobes for mercury ion detection, moreover, it demonstrated excitation-dependent and superior stability in acidic and alkaline media. Besides, the probe exhibited a good linearity range (0.25–50μM) and a low detection limit (13.48nM). These attractive properties indicated that this novel CQDs can adapt to a variety of complex pH environment, which had extensive prospect and promising application for detection of mercury ions in complex water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is the source of life and the link that binds all living beings on this planet. However, contaminations of heavy metals in aquatic environments are of increasing worldwide concerns [1–4]. Among these toxic heavy metals, mercury (Hg(II)) ion is considered as one of the most hazardous toxic heavy-metal contaminant since the extreme toxicity, persistence, and the bioaccumulation [5–8]. The divalent Hg(II) is highly soluble and consequently pollutes the ground and sea water [9,10]. Therefore, the consumption of these waters leads to bioaccumulation and causes the brain damage, affects the liver, kidney and lungs of humans, central nervous system disorders, vision and hearing loss, and Minamata disease [8,11–15]. Indeed, according to the World Health Organization (WHO), the maximum acceptable level of Hg(II) in drinking water is 6μg/L, while the United States Environment Protection Agency recommended the level is 2μg/L [11,16,17]. Therefore, developing a selective, accurate and sensitive analytical method for detecting of Hg(II) in water samples is crucial and challenging for the investigation of water quality.

Up to now, several analytical methods have been described for Hg(II) monitoring and determining. Including atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [18], atomic emission spectroscopy (AES) [19], cold vapor atomic absorption spectroscopy (CV-AAS) [20,21], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [22,23], selective cold vapor-atomic fluorescence spectrometry (CV-AFS) [24,25], surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [26,27], optical and electrochemical sensing devices. Besides, sensing platforms are commonly employed, such as resonance scattering spectrophotometry, electrochemistry, and colorimetry [28–30]. However, there are many disadvantages in the implementation of these methods, such as requirement of expensive and sophisticated instrumentation, time consumption and complex sample pretreatment procedures.

Besides, in the field of selective detection of single metal ion, fluorescence measurement based on fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been rapidly developed in recent years due to its simple operation and low cost. In particularly, detection of Hg2+ by fluorescence CQDs has been reported in many literatures [31–39]. Meanwhile, CQDs exhibits excellent properties including outstanding water solubility, good photostability, low toxicity and ease of preparation [40–44]. Also, most CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. It may be related to the field of energy and storage materials [45–49]. Recently, more resources have been put into preparing fluorescent CQDs via a widely available and harmless hydrothermal method [50–52]. However, most of these fluorescence intensities of CQDs are unstable for changes in pH, the fluorescence intensity dropped significantly when pH is changed, which limits their practical applications in most circumstance [53–58]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a novel CQDs that can adapt to a variety of complex pH environment.

Three million polyacrylamide (PAM) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, dibasic sodium phosphate (Na2HPO4) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Standard stock solutions of Hg2+ were prepared using HgCl2 with ultrapure water. Other stock solutions of metal ions at 100μM were prepared in ultrapure water with the respective salts of AlCl3·6H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, ZnCl2, CuCl2·2H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, SnCl2·2H2O, CdCl2, AgCl, PbCl2, FeCl3·6H2O and FeCl2·4H2O.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs was recorded by a UV-2600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, Japan). Fluorescence measurements were carried out on a G9800A Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). The images of particle CQDs were obtained by a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The functional groups on the CQDs were measured with a TENSOR27 Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Other instruments including a D8-advance X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Bruker, Germany), a Thermo Scientific K-AlphaX photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. U.S.A.), vortex mixer (Hanuo Instrument Co., Ltd., XH-B, Shanghai, China), high speed centrifuge (Shanghai Surgical Instrument Factory, 80–2, Shanghai, China), and vacuum drying oven BPZ-6033 (Shanghai, China) were applied in the whole experiments.

The CQDs prepared through hydrothermal method is described as follows: polyacrylamide (1.0g) was dissolved in deionized water (100mL). The solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (150mL) and heated at 220°C for 5h. After the reaction, the autoclave was taken out from the oven and cooled to room temperature naturally. Then the yellow aqueous solution was purified by filtration through 0.22μm filter and then centrifuged at 10000rpm for 15min to remove large particles, stored at 4°C before using.

The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) can be calculated using the following equation:
QY =
∫
L emission
∫
E solvent
− ∫
E sample
where QY is the absolute quantum yield, Lemission is the photon numbers of the FL emission of CQDs, and Esample and Esolvent are the photon numbers of excitation light used for excitation of CQDs and the solvent (DI water), respectively [61].

The quenching of fluorescence by Hg2+ was described using the Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K sv
C
where Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, C is the concentration of Hg2+, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of CQDs without Hg2+ and with different concentration of Hg2+, respectively. The detection limit used the equation 3σ/m, where σ is the relative standard deviation and m the slope of calibration curve as described in detail [62].

The detection of Hg2+ was performed at room temperature. The standard stock solution of Hg2+ was prepared by dissolving the appropriate amount of HgCl2. 40μL CQDs solution and different amounts of Hg2+ were added into a 10.0mL centrifuge tube, and the mixture was diluted to 4.0mL with ultrapure water and vortex-mixed for 1min to mix completely before spectral measurements. The fluorescence spectra were collected with excitation wavelength at 310nm, and the slit widths of the excitation and emission were both 5nm. In order to assess the influence of co-existing ions on the fluorescence of CQDs and evaluate the selectivity for Hg2+, the similar procedure was performed for the detection of other metal ions: Al3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Sn2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Fe3+, and Fe2+.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) technique was used to explore the morphology and particle size distributions of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A1, the CQDs had high dispersity and uniform spherical shapes, and with a size distribution within the range of 3–10nm and an average diameter of about 5.7nm. Furthermore, Fig. 2A2 shows the EDS pattern of synthesized CQDs. The EDS pattern clearly indicates that carbon accounts for a large proportion in the CQDs. The proportion of oxygen and nitrogen is similar.

The surface chemistry of CQDs was studied using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The typical FT-IR spectra of CQDs was shown in Fig. 2B. As can be seen, a double broad peak at 3430.18cm−1 and 3131.85cm−1 reveals the existence of NH stretching vibrations of amine groups, several sharp peaks at 1631.61cm−1, 1399.58cm−1, 1123.47cm−1 correspond to NH bending vibration, COCH2 bending vibration, and CN bond, respectively. They can be assigned to the existence of amino bending. The absorptions at 619.12cm−1 display CH stretching vibrations, these results indicate the generation of nitrogen-doped CQDs.

The XPS spectrum provided more convincing evidence for elemental contents and surface groups of the prepared CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2C, the full scan XPS spectra presented distinct peaks locating at 284.0, 399.2, and 530.4eV, which are attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. This further corroborated that the prepared CQDs mainly contained carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. The high resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2D) displayed four different types of surface components, corresponding to CC/CC at binding energies of 283.1eV, CN at 284.6eV, CO at 286.9eV, and CO at 287.8eV. In addition, the high resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2E) was fitted with two peaks at 530.1eV and 532.3eV, which are ascribed to CO and CO bonds. The surface components of CQDs determined by the XPS were in agreement with that of FT-IR results. According to the FT-IR and XPS results, nitrogen was codoped in CQDs, and their related functional groups, including carboxyl, acylamide and amino existed on their surface.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was also performed to further investigate the formation of CQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2F, The XRD patterns of CQDs displayed a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ=23° with the interlayer spacing of 0.376nm, which is broader than that of graphite (0.34nm). The broader interlayer space is attributed to richen surface oxygen groups of CQDs. Associated with the graphitic structure, which further confirms their crystalline nature. Therefore, all of the above results demonstrate the existence of CQDs.

To explore the optical properties of the CQDs, UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the CQDs in aqueous solution were investigated as shown in Fig. 3 . It is observed that a characteristic UV–Vis strong absorption peak was located at 290nm (Fig. 3A). The optical absorption peak was typically assigned to the n–π* transition and the contribution of surface moieties. In the prepared CQDs, the electron-rich nature of unsaturated groups with nitrogen atoms changed the charge density of CQDs, resulting in the energy transfer to the excited π* state of the sp2 cluster of CQDs. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 3B, the maximum emission intensity (QY=7.26%) is centered at 410nm with an excitation wavelength of 310nm. The as-prepared CQDs is faint yellow under daylight and emit a brighter blue fluorescence under the excitation of 365nm UV light. Moreover, the CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. As shown in Fig. 3C, when the excitation wavelength decreased from 310nm to 230nm, the fluorescence emission peak was obviously shifted to longer wavelength with variable fluorescence intensity which may be attributed to different sized carbon nanoparticles in the CQDs solution, these excitation tunable emission spectra are considered to be the versatile characteristic of CQDs and this property may be due to quantum confinement, size distribution or the presence of emissive traps on the surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for the Determination of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the optimal fluorescence signal, some critical factors of experimental parameters such as the pH of aqueous solutions, and reaction time were further investigated for obtaining good parameters for the detection of Hg2+ based on the fluorescence quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was investigated under various pH solutions (2.0 to 12.0). It is amazing that CQDs exhibits excellent stability when pH is changed. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs is strong when the pH is higher than 8.0 or less than 4.0, while the fluorescence intensity has a slight drop in pH from 4.0 to 8.0. In the experiments, the amino moieties of acylamino are protonated to ammonium salt (NH3 +) at low pH, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs keeps stable owing to the contribution of carboxyl. Contrarily, the fluorescence intensity keeps stable in alkaline solution because of the role of acylamide, while the carboxyl groups be protonated in alkaline solution. Accordingly, the CQDs has a great advantage that can adapt to a variety of complex pH environment.

To test the response time of the CQDs to Hg2+, Fig. 4B shows the change in fluorescence intensity of CQDs against time after the addition of Hg2+. The fluorescence intensity drops rapidly in the first 10min and then reaches the lowest intensity. Further increase in reaction time hardly causes the change in the fluorescence intensity. Thus, 10min was selected as the optimal reaction time.

In summary, based on optimum conditions, as described in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs linearly decrease with Hg2+ concentrations. The fluorescence quenching data can be fitted by the Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F−1=KSVC, there is a favorable linear correlation (R2 =0.9988) between fluorescence intensity of CQDs and Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0.25 to 50μM, the detection limit of CQDs is about 13.48nM (S/N=3) for Hg2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and Selectivity Measurements and Possible Fluorescence Quenching Mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimum conditions, the sensitivity of CQDs for Hg2+ was investigated. It can be seen in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was obviously quenched along with the increase of the Hg2+ concentration, but had no effect on the shape of fluorescence spectrum, which revealed the sensor was very sensitive to Hg2+. Taking into account the complexity of a real environmental system, an excellent fluorescent probe should not only show high sensitivity but also possess specific selectivity. To investigate the selectivity of CQDs toward different metal ions, including Al3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Sn2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Fe3+, and Fe2+. The fluorescence intensity responses were recorded. As shown in Fig. 5B, as the potential interfering substances, although Cu2+, Ag+ and Fe3+ have a slight impact on fluorescence intensity responses, no obvious fluorescence quenching was observed after the addition of metal ions, except Hg2+. That is, the above results indicated that interferents had no obvious influence on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs except for Cu2+, Ag+ and Fe3+, which can quench the fluorescence slightly. The obvious fluorescence quenching ability may be attributed to the special ion selective chemical structure of CQDs and the inner filter effect. The excellent sensing performance could be probably attributed to the strong affinity between Hg2+ and the carboxylic group or acylamino on the CQDs surface. The amino groups has a positive charge, while the surface of the CQDs had a negative charge because of the carboxyl groups, leading to the close proximity between each other. The nitrogen atom had a lone pair of electrons that could bind to the metal ion to form a complex. Compared with other metal ions, Hg2+ had a larger ionic radius and polarization. Therefore, deformation happened more easily when it interacted with the nitrogen atoms, which may lead to the decrease of the observed fluorescence intensity through absorption, this may be called as inner filter effect. The sensor possessed great sensitivity and selectivity to Hg2+ detection. The fluorescence quenching of CQDs by Hg2+ may be a static quenching process. Therefore, high selectivity of CQDs is due to the more powerful affinity and faster chelating kinetics of Hg2+ than other metal ions toward the carbonyl and amino groups on the surface of the CQDs [63,64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hg2+ Measurement in Real Waters Samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further test whether CQDs could be used in real sample analysis, we used tap water and waste water samples for the practical applications. The tap water was derived from our lab without any pretreatment. The waste water samples were filtered with a 0.22μm membrane, then were added into the CQDs system, and the corresponding fluorescence data were recorded. As listed in Table 1 , it could be clearly seen that the spiked recoveries were in the range of 96.79–105.41% and the RSD (%) was lower than 3.19%. The results revealed that this method is capable of detecting mercury ion in real samples and can be effectively used for further research.

In this study, a novel water-soluble fluorescent CQDs was prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method from polyacrylamide (PAM), which shows excellent selectivity and sensitivity toward Hg2+. More notably, the CQDs has a great advantage that can adapt to a variety of complex pH environment. In addition, a good linear relationship was achieved between the fluorescence response of CQDs and the concentration of Hg2+ in the range from 0.25μM to 50μM. The detection limits was 13.48nM under optimized conditions. The developed method was validated with two real water samples and satisfied spiked recoveries were achieved. Moreover, a simple and fast approach for detecting the concentration of Hg2+ in environment water was developed, demonstrating great potential application in real analysis in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (2016P2013118006), Yunnan Province, China.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: National Primary Drinking Water Regulations: Ground Water Rule; Final Rule. Environmental
